name: Famous Paintings & Artists
description: From the Mona Lisa to The Starry Night — the paintings that changed history. Identify masterpieces by sight, learn the artists and movements behind them, and discover the stories these works tell.

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted this iconic portrait, the most visited artwork in the world?
facts.explanation: Leonardo da Vinci painted the Mona Lisa between c. 1503 and 1519. The subject is believed to be Lisa Gherardini, wife of Florentine merchant Francesco del Giocondo. Leonardo used his sfumato technique — soft, hazy transitions with no sharp outlines — to achieve the painting's dreamlike quality.
facts.statement: The Mona Lisa was painted by Leonardo da Vinci between c. 1503 and 1519.
facts.wowFactor: The Mona Lisa was stolen from the Louvre in 1911 by an Italian handyman who hid it under his coat. It was missing for two years before being recovered.

variants.question: What is the most famous painting by Leonardo da Vinci, currently displayed at the Louvre?

facts.quizQuestion: In which museum is the Mona Lisa permanently displayed?
facts.explanation: The Mona Lisa has been on permanent display at the Musée du Louvre in Paris since 1797. It hangs in Room 711 (Salle des États) and draws over 6 million visitors per year to that room alone.
facts.statement: The Mona Lisa is permanently displayed at the Louvre in Paris.
facts.wowFactor: The Mona Lisa is protected by bulletproof glass and displayed in its own room — 6 million people come just to see it each year.

facts.quizQuestion: What painting technique — creating soft, smoky transitions without sharp outlines — did Leonardo use extensively in the Mona Lisa?
facts.explanation: Sfumato (from Italian 'fumo', smoke) is Leonardo da Vinci's signature technique of blending colors so subtly that edges appear to dissolve. In the Mona Lisa, it creates the mysterious softness around the subject's eyes and smile. Leonardo developed it through his study of optics and human vision.
facts.statement: Leonardo da Vinci used sfumato in the Mona Lisa to create soft, hazy transitions without sharp outlines.
facts.wowFactor: The Mona Lisa has no visible outlines anywhere — Leonardo blended every transition so gradually it approaches the limit of human visual perception.

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted this famous mural depicting the moment Jesus announces his betrayal?
facts.explanation: Leonardo da Vinci painted The Last Supper between c. 1495 and 1498 on the wall of the refectory of Santa Maria delle Grazie in Milan. It depicts the moment Jesus announces 'one of you will betray me' (John 13:21), capturing each apostle's distinct reaction.
facts.statement: The Last Supper was painted by Leonardo da Vinci between c. 1495 and 1498.
facts.wowFactor: The Last Supper is not a true fresco — Leonardo used tempera on dry plaster, which began deteriorating within decades. It survived WWII bombing only because the blast blew away the surrounding building but left the wall standing.

facts.quizQuestion: Where is this iconic fresco by Michelangelo located?
facts.explanation: The Creation of Adam is part of the Sistine Chapel ceiling, painted by Michelangelo between 1508 and 1512. The ceiling covers 1,100 m² and includes 9 scenes from Genesis. The gap between God's and Adam's fingers is one of the most reproduced images in art history.
facts.statement: Michelangelo's Creation of Adam is part of the Sistine Chapel ceiling in Vatican City.
facts.wowFactor: Art historians debate whether the shape surrounding God in the Creation of Adam is anatomically accurate cross-section of a human brain — suggesting Michelangelo hid a tribute to science inside a religious commission.

variants.question: Who painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling, including the famous Creation of Adam?

facts.quizQuestion: In which museum is Botticelli's Birth of Venus displayed?
facts.explanation: Botticelli's Birth of Venus (c. 1484–1486) is in the Uffizi Gallery in Florence, Italy. It depicts Venus emerging from the sea as a fully grown adult, commissioned by the Medici family. Unusually for Italian art of the period, it used tempera on canvas rather than panel.
facts.statement: Botticelli's The Birth of Venus is displayed at the Uffizi Gallery in Florence.
facts.wowFactor: The Birth of Venus was revolutionary for its era — depicting a nude mythological figure was unacceptable in medieval art, but the Medici family commissioned it to celebrate humanist ideals of classical beauty.

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted this fresco depicting ancient Greek philosophers in the Vatican?
facts.explanation: Raphael painted The School of Athens between 1509 and 1511 as part of the Apostolic Palace decoration. It shows over 20 ancient philosophers: Plato and Aristotle are at the center, with Raphael himself appearing in the lower right corner.
facts.statement: The School of Athens was painted by Raphael between 1509 and 1511 in the Vatican.
facts.wowFactor: Raphael modeled Plato on Leonardo da Vinci and Heraclitus on Michelangelo — so three of the greatest Renaissance artists appear in a single painting about ancient Greece.

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted this surreal triptych showing paradise, earthly pleasures, and hell?
facts.explanation: Hieronymus Bosch painted The Garden of Earthly Delights between c. 1490 and 1510. The triptych's three panels depict the Garden of Eden, humanity's earthly pleasures, and Hell. The bizarre symbolic imagery remains partially undeciphered — Bosch had no followers in his own time.
facts.statement: The Garden of Earthly Delights is a triptych painted by Hieronymus Bosch between c. 1490 and 1510.
facts.wowFactor: In 2015, a music scholar discovered actual musical notation written on a sinner's buttocks in the Hell panel of the Garden of Earthly Delights — and a choir performed it.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — The Garden of Earthly Delights

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted this 1434 portrait featuring a famous convex mirror on the back wall?
facts.explanation: Jan van Eyck painted the Arnolfini Portrait in 1434. It depicts an Italian merchant in Bruges and is signed 'Johannes de Eyck fuit hic 1434' above the mirror — 'Jan van Eyck was here 1434'. The convex mirror reflects the entire room, including two additional figures. It is one of the earliest examples of oil painting on panel.
facts.statement: The Arnolfini Portrait was painted by Jan van Eyck in 1434 and is in the National Gallery, London.
facts.wowFactor: Van Eyck's signature 'Johannes de Eyck fuit hic' (Jan van Eyck was here) may mean the painting functioned as a legal document — a painted wedding certificate.

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted this allegory of spring featuring nine mythological figures in an orange grove?
facts.explanation: Botticelli painted Primavera (Spring) in the late 1470s–early 1480s. The painting shows nine mythological figures including Venus, the Three Graces, Mercury, Zephyr, and Chloris. Ernst Gombrich called it 'one of the most written about, and most controversial paintings in the world.' It hangs in the Uffizi Gallery alongside The Birth of Venus.
facts.statement: Primavera was painted by Botticelli in the late 1470s–early 1480s and hangs in the Uffizi Gallery.
facts.wowFactor: Primavera contains over 500 identified plant species and 190 different flower species, all depicted with botanical accuracy — it is effectively a Renaissance field guide to Italian flora.

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted this famous 'tronie' portrait, often called 'the Dutch Mona Lisa'?
facts.explanation: Vermeer painted Girl with a Pearl Earring around 1665. The subject is unknown — this is a 'tronie' (character study of an idealized figure, not a specific person). The so-called pearl may actually be glass or tin. The painting is at the Mauritshuis in The Hague. Tracy Chevalier's 1999 novel and 2003 film brought it enormous renewed fame.
facts.statement: Girl with a Pearl Earring was painted by Vermeer around 1665 and is at the Mauritshuis in The Hague.
facts.wowFactor: The 'pearl' in Girl with a Pearl Earring may not be a pearl at all — a pearl that size would have been worth a fortune. Analysis suggests it could be glass or tin, painted to look precious.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Girl with a Pearl Earring

variants.question: In which museum is Vermeer's 'Girl with a Pearl Earring' displayed?

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted this enormous militia portrait, the pride of Amsterdam's Rijksmuseum?
facts.explanation: Rembrandt painted The Night Watch in 1642. At 363 × 437 cm it is his largest work. Despite its name, it depicts a daytime militia march — the scene darkened over centuries due to varnish accumulation. It was attacked three times: in 1911, 1975, and 1990.
facts.statement: The Night Watch was painted by Rembrandt in 1642 and is at the Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam.
facts.wowFactor: The Night Watch was trimmed on all four sides in 1715 to fit through a door — the original composition, showing two more figures, survives only in a small 17th-century copy.

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted this self-referential Spanish Baroque masterpiece showing the painter at his easel?
facts.explanation: Velázquez painted Las Meninas in 1656. The composition shows the Infanta Margarita with her ladies-in-waiting, two dwarfs, a dog, and Velázquez himself — while the royal couple appears only in a mirror reflection. Pablo Picasso created 58 interpretations of this single painting in 1957.
facts.statement: Las Meninas was painted by Velázquez in 1656 and is in the Prado Museum, Madrid.
facts.wowFactor: Las Meninas creates an unsolvable puzzle: the viewer stands where the king and queen are reflected — meaning you are the subject of the painting, watching Velázquez paint the royal couple.

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted this 1632 group portrait of a public dissection in Amsterdam?
facts.explanation: Rembrandt painted The Anatomy Lesson of Dr. Nicolaes Tulp in 1632, when he was only 26. Commissioned by the Amsterdam Surgeons Guild, it depicts Dr. Tulp performing a public dissection of an executed criminal's arm. The painting launched Rembrandt's career as Amsterdam's most sought-after portraitist.
facts.statement: The Anatomy Lesson of Dr. Nicolaes Tulp was painted by Rembrandt in 1632 and is at the Mauritshuis.
facts.wowFactor: Rembrandt made an anatomical error: Dr. Tulp is dissecting the arm's flexor muscles from the wrong side — yet the painting was so celebrated that no one noticed for centuries.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — The Anatomy Lesson of Dr. Nicolaes Tulp

facts.quizQuestion: Which Baroque artist — one of the first women to gain recognition in post-Renaissance art — painted this visceral beheading scene?
facts.explanation: Artemisia Gentileschi painted Judith Slaying Holofernes around 1612–1613. She was one of the first women to achieve recognition in post-Renaissance art and the first woman admitted to the Accademia delle Arti del Disegno in Florence. The violent scene is interpreted as personal catharsis after her assault by Agostino Tassi in 1611.
facts.statement: Judith Slaying Holofernes (c. 1612–1613) was painted by Artemisia Gentileschi and is in Naples.
facts.wowFactor: Artemisia's version of Judith Slaying Holofernes is considered far more visceral than Caravaggio's — blood spatters, muscles strain — because she lived the story personally.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Judith Slaying Holofernes (Artemisia Gentileschi, Naples)

facts.quizQuestion: Which Baroque artist used a dramatic shaft of light through a dark tavern in this scene of Jesus calling a tax collector?
facts.explanation: Caravaggio painted The Calling of Saint Matthew in 1599–1600 for the Contarelli Chapel in Rome, where it still hangs today. The groundbreaking use of chiaroscuro — a single shaft of light cutting through total darkness — transformed Baroque painting. Jesus's pointing hand echoes Michelangelo's God in the Sistine Chapel.
facts.statement: The Calling of Saint Matthew was painted by Caravaggio in 1599–1600 for the Contarelli Chapel in Rome.
facts.wowFactor: Caravaggio's painting still hangs in its original location — you can visit the Contarelli Chapel in San Luigi dei Francesi, Rome and see it today exactly where it was placed in 1600.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — The Calling of Saint Matthew

facts.quizQuestion: Which Dutch Golden Age master painted this allegory of painting, kept by the artist until his death?
facts.explanation: Vermeer painted The Art of Painting around 1666–1668 and kept it until his death — suggesting he valued it above all his other works. It shows an artist (possibly Vermeer himself, seen from behind) painting a young woman wearing a laurel wreath. Hitler acquired it from the Czernin family in 1940; it was recovered from an Austrian salt mine in 1945.
facts.statement: Vermeer's The Art of Painting (c. 1666–1668) is in the Kunsthistorisches Museum in Vienna.
facts.wowFactor: The Art of Painting was seized by Hitler from an Austrian family in 1940 and hidden in a salt mine. American soldiers found it in 1945 — still rolled up — and returned it to Austria.

facts.quizQuestion: Caravaggio revolutionized Baroque painting with his extreme use of which technique — strong contrasts between light and dark?
facts.explanation: Chiaroscuro (Italian: 'light-dark') uses strong contrasts between light and shadow to create drama and volume. Caravaggio's extreme version, called 'tenebrism', placed most of the painting in deep shadow with a single dramatic light source. Rembrandt and countless Baroque artists adopted this approach.
facts.statement: Caravaggio's extreme use of chiaroscuro (tenebrism) — painting most of the scene in deep shadow with a single dramatic light — revolutionized Baroque painting.
facts.wowFactor: Caravaggio killed a man in a brawl in 1606 and spent his final years as a fugitive across Naples, Malta, and Sicily — his violent life mirrored his violent art.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many pages of notebooks did Leonardo da Vinci leave behind, containing designs for flying machines, armored vehicles, and anatomical studies?
facts.explanation: Leonardo da Vinci left over 13,000 pages of notebooks containing designs for inventions centuries ahead of their time — flying machines, solar power concentrators, armored vehicles, and the double hull. He studied human anatomy through illegal dissections. Most designs were never built.
facts.statement: Leonardo da Vinci left over 13,000 pages of notebooks with designs for flying machines, armored vehicles, and anatomical studies.
facts.wowFactor: Leonardo's notebook designs for a helicopter, solar concentrator, and armored vehicle were so advanced they weren't built until 400+ years after his death.

facts.quizQuestion: Rembrandt painted more self-portraits than almost any artist before the 19th century — approximately how many painted self-portraits did he make?
facts.explanation: Rembrandt painted over 40 self-portraits, documenting his own aging across his career — from young success to bankruptcy and old age. This was unprecedented before the 19th century. His late self-portraits (including Self-Portrait with Two Circles) are considered among the greatest ever painted.
facts.statement: Rembrandt painted over 40 self-portraits, one of the largest bodies of self-portraiture in art history before the 19th century.
facts.wowFactor: Rembrandt's last self-portrait was painted when he was nearly bankrupt — his house, art collection, and belongings had all been auctioned off. The painting shows unflinching psychological honesty.

facts.quizQuestion: Vermeer is celebrated as one of the greatest Dutch masters, yet he left only a tiny body of work. How many paintings are confidently attributed to him?
facts.explanation: Only 34–36 paintings are confidently attributed to Vermeer, who produced roughly 2–3 works per year. He earned his main income as an art dealer. He died leaving his wife Catharina Bolnes in debt with 11 children. He was largely forgotten after his death until rediscovered by critic Théophile Thoré-Bürger in 1866.
facts.statement: Only 34–36 paintings are confidently attributed to Vermeer — an extraordinarily small output for such a celebrated artist.
facts.wowFactor: Vermeer was almost entirely forgotten for nearly 200 years after his death. A French critic rediscovered him in 1866, attributing dozens of unsigned works by analyzing paint style and subject matter.

facts.quizQuestion: Which art movement (early 17th–c. 1750), associated with the Catholic Counter-Reformation, is characterized by grandeur, drama, and chiaroscuro?
facts.explanation: The Baroque movement emerged in Italy in the early 17th century, encouraged by the Catholic Church to counter Protestant simplicity. Key characteristics include grandeur, drama, movement, and emotional intensity. Major Baroque artists include Caravaggio, Rembrandt, Rubens, Velázquez, and Vermeer.
facts.statement: Baroque art (c. early 17th century–c. 1750) is characterized by grandeur, drama, and chiaroscuro, linked to the Counter-Reformation.
facts.wowFactor: The Catholic Church essentially funded the Baroque movement as propaganda — commissioning dramatic, emotional art to win back hearts and minds from Protestant simplicity.

facts.quizQuestion: What art movement (14th–17th century), originating in Italy, revived classical Greek and Roman ideals and pioneered linear perspective?
facts.explanation: The Renaissance began in Italy (Florence, Rome, Venice) and marked the transition from the Middle Ages to modernity. It revived classical antiquity and is associated with Leonardo, Michelangelo, Raphael, and Botticelli. The High Renaissance (~1490–1527) represents its peak.
facts.statement: The Renaissance (broadly 14th–17th century) began in Italy and revived classical Greek and Roman ideals in art, architecture, and learning.
facts.wowFactor: Renaissance artists like Leonardo and Botticelli were funded by the Medici banking family — essentially a private arts patronage empire that shaped all of Western art.

facts.quizQuestion: Which French Romantic painter depicted Liberty as a bare-breasted woman leading the July Revolution of 1830?
facts.explanation: Eugène Delacroix painted Liberty Leading the People in 1830 to commemorate the July Revolution that overthrew King Charles X of France. The central allegorical figure of Liberty (Marianne) wears a Phrygian cap and holds the French tricolour. Delacroix said: 'If I didn't fight for the homeland, I'll paint for her.'
facts.statement: Liberty Leading the People was painted by Delacroix in 1830 and is in the Louvre.
facts.wowFactor: Victor Hugo cited Delacroix's Liberty Leading the People as an inspiration for Les Misérables — the barricade scenes directly echo the painting's imagery.

facts.quizQuestion: Which German Romantic painter created this iconic image of a man contemplating a misty landscape from behind?
facts.explanation: Caspar David Friedrich painted Wanderer above the Sea of Fog around 1818. The back-to-the-viewer composition (Rückenfigur — Friedrich's signature device) was revolutionary — viewers identify with the figure rather than observing him. The painting epitomizes the Romantic concept of the Sublime: nature's power to inspire awe.
facts.statement: Wanderer above the Sea of Fog was painted by Caspar David Friedrich around 1818 and is in the Hamburger Kunsthalle.
facts.wowFactor: Friedrich's Wanderer above the Sea of Fog has been used in hundreds of advertisements, films, and album covers — his 1818 painting arguably defines how we picture a solitary human confronting nature.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Wanderer above the Sea of Fog

facts.quizQuestion: Which Japanese artist created this famous woodblock print depicting Mount Fuji dwarfed by a giant wave?
facts.explanation: Hokusai created The Great Wave off Kanagawa around 1831, published in his series Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji. He was approximately 71 years old at the time. The print became famous in Europe after Japan opened to trade in the 1850s — Monet, Van Gogh, and Debussy all cited its influence. It appears on Japanese passports.
facts.statement: The Great Wave off Kanagawa was created by Hokusai around 1831 as part of his Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji series.
facts.wowFactor: Hokusai created The Great Wave at age 71, when many artists retire. He continued working until age 88, reportedly saying he was just getting good at age 73.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — The Great Wave off Kanagawa

variants.question: What is the art movement of Hokusai's Great Wave off Kanagawa?

facts.quizQuestion: Which Spanish artist painted this harrowing depiction of Napoleon's troops executing Spanish civilians in 1808?
facts.explanation: Francisco Goya painted The Third of May 1808 in 1814, completed six years after the events. It depicts the execution of Spanish civilians by Napoleon's troops the morning after the Dos de Mayo uprising. The white-shirted man with outstretched arms is one of the most iconic martyrdom images in Western art.
facts.statement: The Third of May 1808 was painted by Goya in 1814 and is in the Prado Museum, Madrid.
facts.wowFactor: Goya's Third of May 1808 directly inspired Manet's Execution of Emperor Maximilian and Picasso's Massacre in Korea — creating a chain of anti-war masterpieces across 150 years.

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted this massive Romantic canvas depicting the survivors of an 1816 shipwreck adrift on a raft?
facts.explanation: Théodore Géricault painted The Raft of the Medusa in 1818–1819. The painting depicts the aftermath of the 1816 wreck of the French frigate Méduse — 150 survivors were set adrift; only 15 survived 13 days later with reports of cannibalism. Géricault interviewed survivors and studied cadavers to prepare. He was only 27 when he painted it.
facts.statement: The Raft of the Medusa was painted by Géricault in 1818–1819 and is in the Louvre. The 27-year-old artist died just 5 years later.
facts.wowFactor: Géricault lived with the subject of The Raft of the Medusa — interviewing survivors, visiting morgues, keeping severed limbs in his studio to study decomposition. The painting caused a political scandal when exhibited in 1819.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — The Raft of the Medusa

facts.quizQuestion: Which British Romantic artist painted this blurred locomotive bursting through rain and fog in 1844?
facts.explanation: J. M. W. Turner painted Rain, Steam, and Speed – The Great Western Railway in 1844. It shows a steam locomotive crossing Maidenhead Railway Bridge in fog and rain. Turner is often cited as a precursor to Impressionism for his atmospheric, light-dissolving style. The painting is in the National Gallery, London.
facts.statement: Rain, Steam, and Speed was painted by Turner in 1844 and is in the National Gallery, London.
facts.wowFactor: Turner reportedly stuck his head out of a train window in a rainstorm for 10 minutes to feel the sensation before painting Rain, Steam, and Speed — whether true or legend, it inspired the painting.

facts.quizQuestion: In which museum is Emanuel Leutze's famous painting Washington Crossing the Delaware permanently displayed?
facts.explanation: Leutze's Washington Crossing the Delaware (1851) is at the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York. Ironically, the German-American artist painted it in Düsseldorf, Germany, using Rhine River models. The American flag shown didn't yet exist at the time of the 1776 crossing.
facts.statement: Washington Crossing the Delaware was painted by Leutze in 1851 and is at the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York.
facts.wowFactor: Washington Crossing the Delaware was painted in Germany by a German-American artist using German models and the Rhine River as reference — yet it became one of the most iconic images of American history.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Realist painter caused scandal at the 1865 Paris Salon with this nude woman staring defiantly at the viewer?
facts.explanation: Édouard Manet exhibited Olympia at the 1865 Paris Salon. The scandal was not the nudity itself (mythological nudes were acceptable) but the obvious contemporary setting — the woman is clearly a real sex worker, staring at the viewer with cool defiance. Claude Monet organized a public subscription in 1890 to purchase it for the French state.
facts.statement: Manet's Olympia (1863, exhibited 1865) scandalized Paris because it depicted a real, contemporary nude rather than a mythological figure.
facts.wowFactor: Manet deliberately referenced Titian's Venus of Urbino — same reclining pose — but replaced the faithful dog (symbol of fidelity) with a black cat (symbol of bad luck and prostitution) as a pointed commentary.

facts.correctAnswer: Arrangement in Grey and Black No. 1
facts.quizQuestion: The painting everyone calls 'Whistler's Mother' has a formal title that emphasizes composition over subject — what is it?
facts.explanation: Whistler's formal title is 'Arrangement in Grey and Black No. 1' (1871) — he prioritized aesthetic composition ('art for art's sake') over the identity of the subject. His mother Anna sat as a last-minute replacement for a model who cancelled. It was the first American artwork acquired by France (1891) and is now in the Musée d'Orsay.
facts.statement: The official title of 'Whistler's Mother' is 'Arrangement in Grey and Black No. 1' — Whistler prioritized aesthetic form over the identity of his subject.
facts.wowFactor: Whistler's Mother was featured on a 1934 US postage stamp 'In Memory and Honor of the Mothers of America' — cementing a Victorian portrait as the global icon of motherhood.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Realist painter depicted three peasant women collecting leftover grain — controversial for dignifying the rural poor?
facts.explanation: Jean-François Millet painted The Gleaners in 1857. Critics saw political threat in the dignified depiction of the rural poor (three years after the 1848 revolution). Gleaning was the ancient right of the poor to collect leftover crops after harvest. Millet came from a peasant family himself. The painting is in the Musée d'Orsay.
facts.statement: The Gleaners was painted by Millet in 1857 and is in the Musée d'Orsay. It was controversial for dignifying the rural poor.
facts.wowFactor: The Gleaners was so controversial in 1857 that critics demanded police protection at the exhibition — middle-class Parisians feared its dignified portrayal of poverty was revolutionary propaganda.

facts.quizQuestion: Which art movement (c. 1780–1850), born as a reaction against Enlightenment rationalism, emphasized emotion, imagination, and the Sublime in nature?
facts.explanation: Romanticism emerged in Europe (Germany, France, Britain) in the late 18th century as a reaction against Enlightenment rationalism and the Industrial Revolution. It advocated subjectivity, imagination, emotion, and wild nature. Key artists include Friedrich, Delacroix, Turner, Goya, and Constable.
facts.statement: Romanticism (c. 1780–1850) emphasized emotion, imagination, and the Sublime in nature as a reaction against Enlightenment rationalism.
facts.wowFactor: Romanticism was partly born from fear — the Industrial Revolution was destroying the natural world, and Romantic artists like Friedrich and Turner painted nature as sacred and terrifying before it disappeared.

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted this 1872 work, the painting that gave Impressionism its name?
facts.explanation: Monet painted Impression, Sunrise in 1872, depicting the port of Le Havre at dawn. When exhibited at the First Impressionist Exhibition in 1874, critic Louis Leroy mockingly titled his review 'Exhibition of the Impressionists' — the artists adopted the name proudly. It was stolen from the Marmottan Museum in 1985 and recovered in 1990.
facts.statement: Monet's Impression, Sunrise (1872) gave the Impressionist movement its name when critic Louis Leroy used it mockingly in 1874.
facts.wowFactor: The word 'Impressionism' was an insult — critic Louis Leroy meant to mock the blurry, unfinished look of Monet's sunrise. The artists adopted the name defiantly and it became one of the most beloved art movements in history.

variants.question: Which painting gave the Impressionist movement its name when shown at the first Impressionist Exhibition in 1874?
distractors: Bal du moulin de la Galette

facts.quizQuestion: Where are Monet's large-scale Water Lilies murals — commissioned as a gift to France — permanently displayed?
facts.explanation: Monet's monumental Water Lilies murals (eight large-scale panels) are permanently installed at the Musée de l'Orangerie in Paris, donated to France in 1918 as a commemoration of the WWI armistice. Monet painted approximately 250 Water Lilies works between 1896 and 1926, many while suffering from cataracts.
facts.statement: Monet's large-scale Water Lilies murals are permanently installed at the Musée de l'Orangerie, Paris — donated as a WWI armistice gift to France.
facts.wowFactor: Monet painted hundreds of Water Lilies while nearly blind from cataracts — his later works became increasingly abstract blobs of color, which many critics now see as accidental proto-Abstract Expressionism.

facts.quizQuestion: Seurat's Sunday on La Grande Jatte is the founding work of which technique — thousands of tiny colored dots that mix optically in the viewer's eye?
facts.explanation: Seurat developed pointillism — applying small, distinct dots of pure color that optically mix when viewed from a distance — based on color theory. A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte (1884–1886), at the Art Institute of Chicago, required two years of work and numerous preparatory studies.
facts.statement: Seurat's La Grande Jatte (1884–1886) is the founding work of Pointillism — dots of pure color that optically mix at a distance.
facts.wowFactor: Seurat called his own method 'Chromoluminarism' or 'Divisionism' — the word 'pointillism' was coined by critics and he actually disliked it. La Grande Jatte took 2 years and dozens of preparatory studies.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte

acceptableAlternatives: Museum of Modern Art, New York
facts.quizQuestion: In which museum is Van Gogh's Starry Night — painted from his asylum window — permanently displayed?
facts.explanation: Van Gogh painted The Starry Night in June 1889 from the east-facing window of his room at the Saint-Rémy-de-Provence asylum, where he had voluntarily committed himself after the famous ear-cutting incident. It is now at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in New York, acquired in 1941.
facts.statement: Van Gogh's Starry Night (1889) was painted from his asylum window and is at MoMA in New York.
facts.wowFactor: Van Gogh never considered the Starry Night one of his best works — he wrote to his brother Theo that he was studying its failures. Today it is arguably the most recognized painting of the 19th century.

variants.question: Where was Van Gogh living when he painted The Starry Night in 1889?

facts.quizQuestion: Which museum holds the most famous version of Van Gogh's Sunflowers — painted to decorate Paul Gauguin's bedroom?
facts.explanation: Van Gogh painted his Arles Sunflowers series in 1888 to decorate his Yellow House for Paul Gauguin's visit. The most famous version (the 'Fourth Version') is at the National Gallery in London. Van Gogh painted five versions of the vase-with-sunflowers composition.
facts.statement: The most famous version of Van Gogh's Sunflowers (1888) is at the National Gallery in London. It was painted to decorate his Yellow House for Gauguin's visit.
facts.wowFactor: Van Gogh designed sunflowers to be 'yellow on yellow' — an extreme challenge — to prove to Gauguin he could master color. Gauguin later wrote that the Sunflowers were Van Gogh's greatest achievement.

facts.quizQuestion: In which London museum is Van Gogh's Self-Portrait with Bandaged Ear displayed?
facts.explanation: Van Gogh painted Self-Portrait with Bandaged Ear in January 1889, shortly after he cut off part of his left ear on December 23, 1888, after an argument with Paul Gauguin. The painting is at the Courtauld Gallery in London. It shows Japanese woodblock print influence on the back wall.
facts.statement: Van Gogh painted his Self-Portrait with Bandaged Ear in January 1889 after the ear-cutting incident and it is at the Courtauld Gallery, London.
facts.wowFactor: Van Gogh cut off only part of his ear — the lower lobe — not the whole ear as is commonly believed. He then brought it to a woman named Rachel at a local brothel, reportedly saying 'keep this object carefully.'

facts.quizQuestion: Which Impressionist painted this vibrant Sunday afternoon at a Montmartre dance hall, considered one of Impressionism's greatest masterpieces?
facts.explanation: Renoir painted Bal du moulin de la Galette in 1876 — a large-scale outdoor scene of working-class Parisians dancing and socializing at a Montmartre dance hall. It is considered one of Impressionism's most celebrated masterpieces and is in the Musée d'Orsay.
facts.statement: Bal du moulin de la Galette was painted by Renoir in 1876 and is considered one of Impressionism's greatest masterpieces. It is in the Musée d'Orsay.
facts.wowFactor: Renoir carried the large canvas for Bal du moulin de la Galette back and forth to Montmartre every day so he could paint the real people and real light — refusing to paint it in a studio.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Bal du moulin de la Galette

facts.quizQuestion: In which Washington D.C. museum is Renoir's Luncheon of the Boating Party — named best painting at the 1882 Impressionist Exhibition?
facts.explanation: Renoir's Luncheon of the Boating Party (1881) is at The Phillips Collection in Washington, D.C. It depicts boaters and friends at lunch on the Seine near Paris; Renoir's future wife Aline Charigot appears at lower left playing with a small dog. Three critics named it the best painting at the 1882 Impressionist Exhibition.
facts.statement: Renoir's Luncheon of the Boating Party (1881) is at The Phillips Collection in Washington, D.C. His future wife appears in the lower left.
facts.wowFactor: The Phillips Collection spent a decade pursuing Renoir's Boating Party, finally purchasing it in 1923 for $125,000 — at the time a staggering price that shocked the art world.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Luncheon of the Boating Party

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted this barmaid behind a mirrored counter — his last major work, exhibited at the 1882 Paris Salon?
facts.explanation: Manet painted A Bar at the Folies-Bergère in 1882 — his last major work, exhibited at the Paris Salon that year. It depicts a barmaid named Suzon at the famous Parisian nightclub. The mirror creates deliberate spatial ambiguity: the reflection of the male customer appears where no customer should be. Manet died in 1883.
facts.statement: A Bar at the Folies-Bergère (1882) was Manet's last major work. It is in the Courtauld Gallery, London.
facts.wowFactor: The mirror in Manet's Bar at the Folies-Bergère is deliberately impossible — the reflection doesn't match the scene in front of it. Art historians still debate what Manet intended by this spatial impossibility.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — A Bar at the Folies-Bergère

facts.quizQuestion: In 2011, one version of Cézanne's The Card Players sold for approximately how much — briefly making it the world's most expensive painting?
facts.explanation: One version of Cézanne's The Card Players series sold to the Qatar Royal Family in 2011 for approximately $250 million, briefly setting the record for the most expensive painting ever sold. Cézanne painted five versions of card-game players in Provence in the early 1890s.
facts.statement: A version of Cézanne's The Card Players sold for ~$250 million in 2011 to the Qatar Royal Family, briefly making it the world's most expensive painting.
facts.wowFactor: Cézanne was rejected by the Paris art world for most of his career and painted in isolation in Provence. Today his Card Players series is valued at hundreds of millions — and Picasso and Matisse called him 'the father of modern art.'

facts.quizQuestion: Which Norwegian artist painted this anguished figure against a blood-red sky, a founding image of Expressionism?
facts.explanation: Edvard Munch created The Scream in 1893, one of the most recognized images in Western art. The agonized figure represents existential dread. There are four known versions. The pastel version sold for $119.9 million at Sotheby's in 2012. One oil version was stolen from the National Museum of Norway in 1994 on the same day as the Lillehammer Olympics opening ceremony.
facts.statement: The Scream was created by Edvard Munch in 1893. Four versions exist; the primary is in Oslo's National Museum.
facts.wowFactor: The Scream was stolen from Norway's National Museum in 1994 — on the opening day of the Lillehammer Winter Olympics. A note left at the scene read: 'Thanks for the poor security.'

variants.question: What nationality was Edvard Munch, painter of The Scream?

facts.quizQuestion: Which Impressionist — famous for over 1,500 works depicting dancers — painted this ballet rehearsal scene?
facts.explanation: Edgar Degas painted The Dance Class in 1874. He is best known for his depictions of ballet dancers — over 1,500 works on the subject across his career. Degas captured the rigorous professional reality of ballet, not just performance glamour. He preferred to call himself a Realist rather than an Impressionist.
facts.statement: The Dance Class (1874) was painted by Degas, who made over 1,500 works depicting ballet dancers.
facts.wowFactor: Degas captured ballet dancers in unglamorous moments — scratching their backs, yawning, adjusting shoes — to show the professional reality behind the performance. Contemporary audiences found this scandalously unrefined.

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted this night view of Arles with gas lamp reflections on the Rhône — distinct from the more famous 1889 Starry Night?
facts.explanation: Van Gogh painted Starry Night Over the Rhône in September 1888 from the bank of the Rhône near his Yellow House in Arles. It depicts the gas-lit city reflected in the river. This is a different painting from the 1889 The Starry Night (MoMA) painted from his asylum window. It is in the Musée d'Orsay.
facts.statement: Van Gogh painted Starry Night Over the Rhône in September 1888 from the riverbank in Arles. It is in the Musée d'Orsay.
facts.wowFactor: Van Gogh painted Starry Night Over the Rhône just two months before the ear-cutting incident with Gauguin — a moment of joy and creative productivity that ended in tragedy.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Starry Night Over the Rhône

facts.quizQuestion: Which Austrian Art Nouveau artist painted this gold-leaf embrace — the climax of his 'Golden Period'?
facts.explanation: Gustav Klimt painted The Kiss between 1907 and 1908 using oil paint with gold leaf and silver and platinum on canvas. It was originally exhibited under the title 'Liebespaar' (Lovers). The painting is in the Belvedere Palace in Vienna and is considered the climax of Klimt's ornamental 'Golden Period' (c. 1899–1910).
facts.statement: Gustav Klimt painted The Kiss (1907–1908) using gold leaf in his signature Art Nouveau Golden Period style. It is in the Belvedere, Vienna.
facts.wowFactor: The Kiss was acquired by the Austrian state immediately when it was first exhibited in 1908 — the government paid Klimt on the spot, before the show was even over. It has never left Austria.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Post-Impressionist artist traveled to Tahiti seeking 'ecstasy, calm, and art' — and painted this beach scene in 1891?
facts.explanation: Paul Gauguin painted Tahitian Women on the Beach in 1891 during his first stay on Tahiti. He traveled there seeking to escape European materialism and find a primitive authenticity. The painting is in the Musée d'Orsay. Gauguin's Tahitian works pioneered bold, flat color that influenced 20th-century art.
facts.statement: Gauguin painted Tahitian Women on the Beach in 1891 during his first stay on Tahiti, seeking to escape European materialism.
facts.wowFactor: Gauguin sold everything he owned, left his wife and five children, and moved to Tahiti at age 43 in search of 'primitive' art. His letters reveal a man perpetually disappointed to find French colonialism had already transformed the island.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Tahitian Women on the Beach

facts.quizQuestion: Van Gogh only ever sold one painting during his lifetime — which one?
facts.explanation: Vincent van Gogh sold only one painting during his lifetime: The Red Vineyard (1888), sold in Brussels in 1890 for 400 Belgian francs (approximately 400 French francs). Van Gogh died just months after this sale. He created approximately 2,100 artworks and is now among the most expensive artists at auction.
facts.statement: Van Gogh sold only one painting during his lifetime: The Red Vineyard (1888), sold in 1890 for 400 francs.
facts.wowFactor: Van Gogh sold exactly one painting in his lifetime. Today his works sell for over $100 million at auction. He died never knowing his art would change the course of Western painting.

facts.quizQuestion: Which French art movement (1860s–1880s) rejected official Salon norms in favor of visible brushstrokes, natural light, and everyday subjects?
facts.explanation: Impressionism emerged in France in the 1860s, pioneered by Monet, Renoir, Degas, Berthe Morisot, and others. The movement rejected the official Paris Salon and organized 8 independent exhibitions between 1874 and 1886. The name came mockingly from Monet's Impression, Sunrise.
facts.statement: Impressionism (1860s–1880s) pioneered visible brushstrokes, natural light, and everyday subjects — rejected by the Paris Salon and self-organized in 8 independent exhibitions.
facts.wowFactor: The Impressionists were so rejected by the official Paris Salon that they organized their own rival exhibition in 1874. It was initially mocked, but their movement completely changed the direction of Western art.

facts.quizQuestion: American Gothic is commonly misread — who are the two figures actually depicted according to Grant Wood?
facts.explanation: Grant Wood painted American Gothic in 1930. The subjects are actually the artist's sister Nan Wood Graham and dentist Byron McKeeby as models, and Wood intended them as a farmer and his daughter — not husband and wife as widely assumed. The painting won a $300 prize at the Art Institute of Chicago and is permanently in the AIC collection.
facts.statement: In American Gothic (1930), Grant Wood depicted a farmer and his daughter (not wife) — though it is commonly misread as a married couple.
facts.wowFactor: Grant Wood's sister Nan Wood Graham spent decades correcting the record: 'Everyone thinks I'm the wife. I'm the daughter!' She lived to 94, long enough to correct the misreading publicly many times.

variants.question: Where is Grant Wood's American Gothic permanently displayed?

facts.quizQuestion: In which museum is Hopper's Nighthawks — an icon of urban loneliness — permanently displayed?
facts.explanation: Edward Hopper painted Nighthawks in 1942, depicting four people in a downtown diner late at night, viewed through large plate-glass windows. The fluorescent light against a dark, deserted street became an icon of urban isolation. The Art Institute of Chicago purchased it in 1942 for $3,000.
facts.statement: Hopper's Nighthawks (1942) is at the Art Institute of Chicago. The diner was inspired by a Greenwich Avenue location in New York.
facts.wowFactor: Hopper's Nighthawks diner has no visible door — there is no way in or out of the painting. Art historians debate whether this was deliberate, making the patrons effectively trapped in their loneliness.

facts.quizQuestion: Picasso's Guernica (1937) was painted in response to which atrocity?
facts.explanation: Picasso painted Guernica in 1937 in response to the Nazi Condor Legion's bombing of the Basque town of Guernica on April 26, 1937, during the Spanish Civil War. The black-and-white painting depicts suffering: a gored horse, a bull, screaming women, a dead baby, and dismembered soldiers. It is at the Museo Reina Sofía in Madrid.
facts.statement: Picasso painted Guernica (1937) in response to the Nazi bombing of the Basque town of Guernica during the Spanish Civil War.
facts.wowFactor: When a Nazi officer demanded 'Did you do this?' pointing at a photo of Guernica, Picasso replied: 'No — you did.' The painting was kept out of Spain until after Franco's death in 1975.

facts.quizQuestion: Who painted The Persistence of Memory — famous for its melting watches in a dreamscape?
facts.explanation: Salvador Dalí painted The Persistence of Memory in 1931. The tiny painting (24.1 × 33 cm — postcard-sized) depicts melting watches in the landscape of Port Lligat, Catalonia. Dalí said the image came to him while staring at a melting Camembert cheese. First sold for $250 in 1932; now at MoMA in New York.
facts.statement: The Persistence of Memory (1931) was painted by Dalí and is at MoMA. Its melting watches were inspired, Dalí said, by a melting Camembert cheese.
facts.wowFactor: The Persistence of Memory is surprisingly tiny — about the size of a postcard (24 × 33 cm). Most visitors are shocked by how small it is compared to its enormous cultural presence.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Pop Art pioneer created Campbell's Soup Cans — a series of 32 paintings depicting every variety of Campbell's soup?
facts.explanation: Andy Warhol created Campbell's Soup Cans in 1961–1962, depicting all 32 varieties of Campbell's soup then available. First exhibited at Ferus Gallery, Los Angeles in July 1962 for $100 each. The work challenged distinctions between fine art and commercial imagery. MoMA acquired all 32 in 1996.
facts.statement: Andy Warhol created Campbell's Soup Cans (1961–62) — 32 paintings of every soup variety, sold for $100 each and now at MoMA.
facts.wowFactor: Warhol originally sold each Campbell's Soup canvas for $100. MoMA eventually acquired all 32. Today they are collectively worth hundreds of millions of dollars.

facts.quizQuestion: Picasso's Les Demoiselles d'Avignon (1907) is considered the proto-founding work of which movement?
facts.explanation: Picasso's Les Demoiselles d'Avignon (1907) is at MoMA and is considered proto-Cubist — a revolutionary departure from Western pictorial tradition. Five nude figures are depicted in angular, disjointed forms with faces influenced by African/Iberian masks. Picasso kept it private for years; not publicly exhibited until 1916.
facts.statement: Picasso's Les Demoiselles d'Avignon (1907) is considered a proto-Cubist work that directly inspired the Cubist movement.
facts.wowFactor: Les Demoiselles d'Avignon was so shocking that even Picasso's close friends — including Matisse and Braque — thought he had lost his mind when he showed it to them privately in 1907.

facts.quizQuestion: What French phrase is inscribed below the pipe in Magritte's The Treachery of Images — making a philosophical point about representation?
facts.explanation: René Magritte's The Treachery of Images (1929, LACMA) bears the inscription 'Ceci n'est pas une pipe' (This is not a pipe). Magritte's point: a painting of a pipe is not a pipe — an image represents an object, it is not the object itself. This philosophical puzzle became a classic example of Surrealist questioning of reality.
facts.statement: Magritte's The Treachery of Images (1929) bears the inscription 'Ceci n'est pas une pipe' — making a philosophical point that a painting of a pipe is not a pipe.
facts.wowFactor: Magritte's 'This is not a pipe' was a philosophical bomb: it challenged the entire tradition of realistic painting by asking what a picture actually IS — a question that the art world is still discussing a century later.

facts.quizQuestion: Jackson Pollock's revolutionary technique of pouring and dripping liquid paint onto horizontal canvas is known as what?
facts.explanation: Jackson Pollock developed his 'drip technique' or 'action painting' — pouring and dripping liquid paint onto a horizontal canvas laid on the floor, moving around it using his whole body. His painting No. 5, 1948 sold for $140 million in 2006. Life magazine's 1949 article 'Is He the Greatest Living Painter?' brought him widespread fame.
facts.statement: Pollock's drip/action painting technique — pouring liquid paint onto a horizontal canvas — was revolutionary for Abstract Expressionism.
facts.wowFactor: Pollock laid his canvases on the floor and walked around them, dripping and pouring paint from cans and sticks — never touching the canvas with a brush. Critics asked if it was art; today his paintings sell for $140 million.

facts.quizQuestion: In 2018, a Banksy work shredded itself through a hidden mechanism immediately after being auctioned for £1.04 million — what was the shredded piece then renamed?
facts.explanation: In October 2018 at Sotheby's London, a framed canvas print of Banksy's Girl with Balloon shredded itself immediately after the hammer fell at £1.04 million — Banksy had hidden a shredder inside the frame years earlier. The partially shredded work was renamed 'Love is in the Bin.' It subsequently sold in 2021 for £18.6 million — 18x more than before the shredding.
facts.statement: Banksy's Girl with Balloon shredded itself at Sotheby's in 2018 after selling for £1.04 million. Renamed 'Love is in the Bin', it sold in 2021 for £18.6 million.
facts.wowFactor: Banksy had hidden a shredder inside the frame of Girl with Balloon years before the auction — waiting. The shredding was perfectly timed to activate when the hammer fell. The art world did not see it coming.

facts.quizQuestion: Frida Kahlo's The Two Fridas (1939) — depicting two versions of herself with exposed hearts — was painted in response to what event?
facts.explanation: Frida Kahlo painted The Two Fridas in 1939, during and after her divorce from muralist Diego Rivera. It shows two Kahlos holding hands: one in a European dress (the rejected Frida) and one in a traditional Tehuana dress (the loved Frida), hearts exposed and connected by an artery that is cut and dripping blood on the European side. It is in the Museo de Arte Moderno, Mexico City.
facts.statement: The Two Fridas (1939) by Frida Kahlo represents her emotional state during and after her divorce from Diego Rivera.
facts.wowFactor: The Two Fridas was Kahlo's first large-scale work. Her husband Diego Rivera later bought it back from Mexico's government for more than its purchase price — calling it her greatest painting.

facts.quizQuestion: Which artist — born in Moscow, who taught at the Bauhaus — is credited as one of the pioneers of pure abstract art in the West?
facts.explanation: Wassily Kandinsky is generally credited as one of the pioneers of abstraction in Western art. He believed colors and shapes could evoke emotions like music. His Composition VIII (1923) is at the Guggenheim Museum in New York. He taught at the Bauhaus in Weimar and Dessau before dying in Paris in 1944.
facts.statement: Wassily Kandinsky is credited as a pioneer of Western abstract art, believing colors and shapes could evoke emotions like music.
facts.wowFactor: Kandinsky reportedly had synesthesia — he could 'hear' colors and 'see' sounds. He described his paintings in musical terms: 'Yellow has the quality of a trumpet; blue is like a cello.'

distractors: Composition II in Red, Blue, and Yellow
facts.quizQuestion: Which Mondrian painting — inspired by New York's street grid and jazz rhythms, painted after he fled WWII — replaced his signature black lines with yellow?
facts.explanation: Piet Mondrian painted Broadway Boogie Woogie in 1942–1943 after moving to New York in 1940 to escape WWII. Inspired by the city's grid of streets and boogie-woogie jazz, he replaced his characteristic black lines with yellow and added small colored squares. It is at MoMA. He died in 1944, just after completing his unfinished 'Victory Boogie Woogie.'
facts.statement: Mondrian's Broadway Boogie Woogie (1942–43, MoMA) was inspired by New York's street grid and jazz — his final departure from strict Neoplasticism.
facts.wowFactor: Mondrian was 68 years old when he moved to New York fleeing the Nazis, fell in love with boogie-woogie jazz, and painted his most joyful work. He died of pneumonia just a year later.

facts.quizQuestion: Which mid-20th-century art movement — pioneered by Warhol, Lichtenstein, and Hamilton — elevated advertising, consumer goods, and celebrities to fine art status?
facts.explanation: Pop Art emerged in the UK and US in the mid-to-late 1950s. It challenged fine art traditions by incorporating imagery from popular/mass culture: advertising, comic strips, product packaging, and celebrities. UK pioneers included Richard Hamilton and David Hockney; US pioneers included Warhol, Roy Lichtenstein, and Jasper Johns.
facts.statement: Pop Art emerged in the UK and US in the late 1950s, elevating commercial imagery — advertising, celebrity, consumer goods — to the status of fine art.
facts.wowFactor: Pop Art's central question — 'Is advertising as worthy as high art?' — challenged centuries of Western art hierarchy. Warhol's answer was to put Campbell's soup next to the Mona Lisa.

facts.quizQuestion: Which painting technique involves applying paint so thickly that the brush or knife strokes are clearly visible in the finished work — creating actual physical texture?
facts.explanation: Impasto (Italian: 'paste') involves laying paint on so thickly that strokes of a brush or painting knife are clearly visible. When dry, it provides actual physical texture — the paint appears to protrude from the canvas. Van Gogh used extremely thick impasto. Also used by Rembrandt, Titian, Velázquez, and Monet.
facts.statement: Impasto is a technique of applying paint very thickly so brush and knife strokes remain visible, creating physical texture on the canvas.
facts.wowFactor: Van Gogh's Starry Night has visible ridges of paint up to 7mm thick — if you could touch the canvas, you would feel the swirling brushstrokes under your fingers.

facts.quizQuestion: What does the French term 'trompe-l'oeil' literally mean in English?
facts.explanation: Trompe-l'oeil (French) literally means 'deceives the eye.' The technique creates a hyper-realistic illusion of three-dimensional space on a flat surface. Ancient Greek painter Zeuxis was said to have painted grapes so realistic that birds pecked at them. Common in Baroque ceiling paintings and Flemish still life.
facts.statement: Trompe-l'oeil literally means 'deceives the eye' in French — a technique creating such realistic illusions that viewers perceive painted objects as real.
facts.wowFactor: In competitive ancient Greek painting, Zeuxis and Parrhasius challenged each other: Zeuxis painted grapes so real birds tried to eat them. Parrhasius painted a curtain so convincing that Zeuxis tried to pull it aside — and lost.

facts.quizQuestion: True fresco (buon fresco) involves painting on what surface while it is still fresh — bonding pigment permanently as it dries?
facts.explanation: Buon fresco ('good fresco') involves painting on fresh, wet plaster — as the plaster dries, the pigment bonds chemically to the wall, becoming permanent. Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel ceiling is buon fresco. The artist must work quickly before the plaster dries. Leonardo's Last Supper used a different technique (tempera on dry plaster) and began deteriorating quickly.
facts.statement: True fresco (buon fresco) involves painting on wet plaster — pigment bonds chemically as it dries, making it highly durable. Michelangelo's Sistine ceiling is buon fresco.
facts.wowFactor: Michelangelo had to plan the Sistine Chapel ceiling in small sections (giornate — 'day's work') because each patch of plaster had to be painted before it dried. A single mistake meant chipping off and replastering.

facts.quizQuestion: Which early Flemish painter is credited with helping develop and popularize the oil painting technique on panel in the early 15th century?
facts.explanation: Jan van Eyck helped develop and popularize oil painting on panel in the early 15th century. His technique — layering thin, transparent glazes of oil-based paint — allowed extraordinary detail and luminosity unseen in tempera. The Arnolfini Portrait (1434) and The Ghent Altarpiece are prime examples.
facts.statement: Jan van Eyck helped develop and popularize oil painting in the early 15th century, enabling extraordinary detail and luminosity.
facts.wowFactor: Van Eyck's oil paint technique was so advanced that 15th-century artists believed he had invented oil painting entirely. Closer study shows he perfected a technique already in development — but his mastery was so complete it looked like invention.

facts.quizQuestion: Who wrote the Surrealist Manifesto in 1924, theorizing that art should express the unconscious mind?
facts.explanation: André Breton wrote the Surrealist Manifesto in 1924, naming and theorizing the Surrealist movement. Surrealism aimed to resolve the contradictory conditions of dream and reality into 'surreality,' allowing the unconscious mind to express itself through illogical, dreamlike imagery. Key artists include Dalí, Magritte, Ernst, Miró, and Kahlo.
facts.statement: André Breton wrote the Surrealist Manifesto in 1924, founding the Surrealist movement which aimed to express the unconscious mind through dreamlike imagery.
facts.wowFactor: Breton was an autocratic founder — he literally expelled members from the Surrealist movement when he disagreed with them, issuing expulsion notices like a general. Even Dalí was eventually expelled.

facts.quizQuestion: Which two artists co-founded Cubism in Paris around 1907–1908?
facts.explanation: Cubism was co-founded by Pablo Picasso and Georges Braque in Paris around 1907–1914. Objects were analyzed and reassembled in abstract form, showing multiple viewpoints simultaneously. Two phases: Analytic Cubism (monochromatic, fragmented) and Synthetic Cubism (collage, brighter colors). Key works: Picasso's Les Demoiselles d'Avignon and Braque's Houses at l'Estaque.
facts.statement: Cubism was co-founded by Picasso and Georges Braque in Paris around 1907–1914, showing objects from multiple viewpoints simultaneously.
facts.wowFactor: Picasso and Braque worked so closely developing Cubism that they later said they couldn't tell their own paintings apart. They were so intertwined creatively that some unsigned works are still disputed between them.

facts.quizQuestion: Abstract Expressionism was the first major art movement to originate in which country, shifting the global center of modern art after WWII?
facts.explanation: Abstract Expressionism emerged in the United States (New York City — the 'New York School') in the 1940s–50s, the first major American art movement to gain international influence. It shifted the global center of modern art from Paris to New York after WWII. Key figures: Pollock, Kline, Gorky, de Kooning, Rothko, Newman.
facts.statement: Abstract Expressionism was the first major American art movement to gain international influence, shifting the global art center from Paris to New York after WWII.
facts.wowFactor: Some art historians argue that the US government secretly promoted Abstract Expressionism during the Cold War through the CIA — funding exhibitions abroad to contrast American 'free' art with Soviet Socialist Realism.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Post-Impressionist artist developed Pointillism in the 1880s — applying thousands of tiny dots of pure color to optically mix in the viewer's eye?
facts.explanation: Georges Seurat developed pointillism (which he called Chromoluminarism or Divisionism) in the 1880s, together with Paul Signac. The technique applies small, distinct dots of pure color that optically mix at a viewing distance, based on color theory. La Grande Jatte (1884–1886) is the founding masterpiece.
facts.statement: Georges Seurat developed Pointillism — tiny dots of pure color that mix optically at a distance — in the 1880s. He preferred to call it 'Chromoluminarism.'
facts.wowFactor: Seurat hated the word 'pointillism' — coined by critics to mock the tiny dots. He insisted on 'Divisionism' or 'Chromoluminarism' to emphasize the scientific color theory behind it.

facts.quizQuestion: The Italian painting technique 'sfumato' gets its name from the Italian word for what?
facts.explanation: Sfumato comes from Italian 'fumo' (smoke), literally meaning 'vanished like smoke.' The technique creates soft, hazy transitions between colors with no distinct outlines — mimicking how the human eye perceives the periphery of vision. Leonardo da Vinci was the most prominent practitioner, using it in the Mona Lisa.
facts.statement: Sfumato comes from Italian 'fumo' (smoke) — the technique creates soft, hazy transitions without distinct outlines, as if the image dissolves like smoke.
facts.wowFactor: Leonardo described sfumato as painting 'without lines or borders, in the manner of smoke or beyond the focus plane' — he based it on scientific study of how the human eye perceives out-of-focus areas.

facts.quizQuestion: Expressionism — presenting the world from a subjective perspective, distorting reality for emotional effect — primarily originated in which country?
facts.explanation: Expressionism emerged primarily in Germany in the early 20th century, peaking around 1905–1920s. Key groups: Die Brücke (Dresden, 1905) and Der Blaue Reiter (Munich, 1911). Key artists: Ernst Ludwig Kirchner, Emil Nolde, Wassily Kandinsky, Franz Marc, Egon Schiele. Munch's The Scream (1893, Norwegian) is considered proto-Expressionist.
facts.statement: Expressionism (peak c. 1905–1920s) primarily emerged in Germany through groups Die Brücke and Der Blaue Reiter, distorting reality to express subjective emotion.
facts.wowFactor: German Expressionism was declared 'degenerate art' (Entartete Kunst) by the Nazis in 1937 — they confiscated 20,000 works and burned many. The 1937 'Degenerate Art' exhibition was meant to mock it, but drew 3 million visitors.

facts.quizQuestion: Which powerful Florentine banking family commissioned Botticelli's Primavera and Birth of Venus, and patronized Leonardo and Michelangelo — essentially funding the Renaissance?
facts.explanation: The Medici family of Florence were the dominant patrons of the Italian Renaissance. They commissioned Botticelli's Primavera and Birth of Venus, supported Leonardo da Vinci's early career, and funded Michelangelo's training. As bankers to the Catholic Church, their wealth flowed into art, architecture, and scholarship.
facts.statement: The Medici banking family of Florence funded much of the Italian Renaissance, commissioning Botticelli, supporting Leonardo, and training Michelangelo.
facts.wowFactor: The Medici were so dominant in Florence that Cosimo de' Medici effectively ran the city while holding no official office — controlling everything through money, marriage, and artistic patronage.

facts.quizQuestion: The Impressionist technique 'en plein air' means painting where?
facts.explanation: En plein air (French: 'in the open air') means painting outdoors, directly observing the subject in natural light. This was central to Impressionism — Monet, Renoir, and Pissarro painted outside to capture the changing effects of natural light. The invention of portable paint in metal tubes (1840s) made plein air painting practical.
facts.statement: En plein air means painting outdoors in natural light — central to Impressionism's goal of capturing the changing quality of natural light.
facts.wowFactor: Before portable paint tubes were invented in the 1840s, oil painters couldn't paint outdoors — paint was stored in bladders and too unwieldy. The humble metal paint tube made Impressionism possible.

facts.quizQuestion: The Dutch Golden Age of painting (17th century) — producing Rembrandt, Vermeer, and Hals — took place in which country?
facts.explanation: The Dutch Golden Age (roughly 1600s–1670s) was a period of extraordinary cultural and economic achievement in the Dutch Republic (now the Netherlands). It produced Rembrandt, Vermeer, Frans Hals, Jan Steen, and Pieter de Hooch. Dutch Golden Age painting specialized in domestic interiors, portraits, landscapes, and still lifes — reflecting the wealthy merchant middle class.
facts.statement: The Dutch Golden Age of painting (17th century) took place in the Dutch Republic (now the Netherlands), producing Rembrandt, Vermeer, and Frans Hals.
facts.wowFactor: The Dutch Golden Age produced so much art that the Dutch Republic had more paintings per capita than anywhere else in Europe — art was a middle-class commodity, not just for royalty and church.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how long did Michelangelo take to paint the entire Sistine Chapel ceiling (1,100 m²)?
facts.explanation: Michelangelo painted the entire Sistine Chapel ceiling — some 1,100 m² — in approximately four years (1508–1512). Despite the popular myth, he painted standing on scaffolding, not lying on his back. Pope Julius II commissioned the work; Michelangelo initially refused, preferring sculpture.
facts.statement: Michelangelo painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling (1,100 m²) in approximately 4 years (1508–1512), standing on scaffolding.
facts.wowFactor: Michelangelo hated the Sistine Chapel commission and complained bitterly throughout. He wrote a satirical sonnet describing the physical agony of painting overhead: 'My beard turns up to heaven; my nape falls in, fixed on my spine...'

facts.quizQuestion: At what age did Raphael die — the same age as Van Gogh — leaving an enormous legacy after just 20 productive years?
facts.explanation: Raphael died at age 37 in 1520, on his birthday. He had accomplished a staggering amount in a short career: the Vatican Stanze frescoes (School of Athens), over 300 paintings and drawings, architectural work on St. Peter's Basilica, and was considered the greatest living painter of his day. He died suddenly, possibly of fever.
facts.statement: Raphael died at age 37 in 1520 — the same age as Van Gogh — leaving the School of Athens, hundreds of paintings, and a legacy as the most admired painter of the High Renaissance.
facts.wowFactor: Raphael was so beloved that when he died, his body lay in state in his studio. The Pope wept. The entire Vatican closed in mourning. No artist before or since received a state funeral of such magnitude.

facts.quizQuestion: In which Madrid museum is Bosch's Garden of Earthly Delights — and Velázquez's Las Meninas — both permanently displayed?
facts.explanation: Both Bosch's Garden of Earthly Delights and Velázquez's Las Meninas are at the Museo del Prado in Madrid. The Prado is one of the world's great art museums, holding exceptional Spanish art alongside key works from Flemish, Italian, and German masters.
facts.statement: The Prado Museum in Madrid holds both Bosch's Garden of Earthly Delights and Velázquez's Las Meninas.
facts.wowFactor: The Prado has over 20,000 works in its collection but displays only about 1,800 at a time — most are in storage. Spain's royal collection was so vast that the museum's hidden depths rival its famous galleries.

facts.quizQuestion: Leonardo da Vinci was one of history's greatest minds — yet how many completed paintings did he leave behind?
facts.explanation: Leonardo da Vinci completed fewer than 20 paintings in his entire life — many were left unfinished as he moved from project to project. His small but extraordinary output includes the Mona Lisa, The Last Supper, Lady with an Ermine, Virgin of the Rocks, and The Annunciation.
facts.statement: Leonardo da Vinci completed fewer than 20 paintings — many were abandoned unfinished as he moved between projects, commissions, and inventions.
facts.wowFactor: Leonardo is estimated to have started hundreds of projects but finished almost none — his patrons constantly complained. The Mona Lisa itself took 16 years and was never officially 'delivered' to the patron who commissioned it.

facts.correctAnswer: He killed a man in a brawl
facts.quizQuestion: Why did Caravaggio spend his final years as a fugitive — fleeing through Naples, Malta, and Sicily?
facts.explanation: Caravaggio killed Ranuccio Tomassoni in a brawl in 1606 — possibly over a disputed tennis match — and was sentenced to death in absentia by the Pope. He spent his final four years as a fugitive through Naples, Malta, and Sicily, dying at 38, possibly of lead poisoning or fever, while trying to return to Rome.
facts.statement: Caravaggio killed a man in 1606 and spent his final years as a fugitive under a papal death sentence, dying at 38 while attempting to return to Rome.
facts.wowFactor: Caravaggio painted some of his greatest works while fleeing a death sentence — in Malta he created The Beheading of St. John, one of his largest works, but was expelled from the Knights of Malta before it was completed.

facts.quizQuestion: Francisco Goya — who painted The Third of May 1808 — was a court painter for which country's royal family?
facts.explanation: Francisco Goya (1746–1828) was court painter to the Spanish Crown, serving kings Carlos III, Carlos IV, and briefly Fernando VII. Despite his royal position, he documented the horrors of Napoleon's occupation of Spain with unflinching honesty. His later 'Black Paintings' show extreme psychological darkness.
facts.statement: Francisco Goya was court painter to the Spanish royal family while simultaneously creating brutally honest anti-war paintings like The Third of May 1808.
facts.wowFactor: Goya painted nightmarish, violent images on the interior walls of his own home (the 'Black Paintings') — including Saturn Devouring His Son — apparently not intended to be seen by anyone. They were discovered after his death.

acceptableAlternatives: Thirty Six Views of Mount Fuji
distractors: One Hundred Famous Views of Edo
facts.quizQuestion: The Great Wave off Kanagawa is the first print in which famous series by Hokusai?
facts.explanation: The Great Wave off Kanagawa is the first print in Hokusai's 'Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji' (c. 1830–1833), which ultimately contained 46 prints (10 extra were added due to its popularity). The series shows Mount Fuji from different locations across Japan in different seasons.
facts.statement: The Great Wave off Kanagawa is the first print in Hokusai's 'Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji' (c. 1830–33) — which actually contains 46 prints.
facts.wowFactor: Hokusai's series was supposed to have 36 views of Mount Fuji, but it was so popular that he added 10 more — making 46. No one complained.
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji

facts.quizQuestion: Which mid-19th-century French art movement rejected Romanticism's idealism in favor of depicting contemporary life, workers, and the poor with unflinching honesty?
facts.explanation: Realism emerged in France in the 1840s–1860s as a reaction against Romantic idealism. Key artists: Gustave Courbet, Jean-François Millet, Honoré Daumier, and early Manet. Realists depicted contemporary subjects — peasants, workers, the urban poor — with truthfulness rather than idealization.
facts.statement: Realism (c. 1840s–1860s) rejected Romantic idealism to depict contemporary life — workers, peasants, the poor — with truthful accuracy.
facts.wowFactor: Courbet's Realist manifesto declared: 'I have never seen an angel. Show me an angel and I'll paint one.' His 1855 self-funded 'Pavilion of Realism' was the first artist to mount their own solo exhibition.

facts.quizQuestion: In which French village did Monet spend the last 43 years of his life, creating the water lily garden that inspired his most famous paintings?
facts.explanation: Claude Monet bought a house at Giverny in Normandy, France in 1883 and lived there until his death in 1926. He designed and created an elaborate water garden with a Japanese bridge, which became the subject of his Water Lilies series — approximately 250 paintings. The garden at Giverny is now a museum open to visitors.
facts.statement: Monet spent his last 43 years at Giverny, France, creating the water garden that inspired his famous Water Lilies series.
facts.wowFactor: Monet designed the Giverny garden as a living artwork — planting it precisely for the way colors and reflections would appear at different times of day and seasons. The garden itself is considered one of his masterpieces.

facts.quizQuestion: Cézanne is called 'the father of modern art' because his geometric approach to form directly influenced the birth of which 20th-century movement?
facts.explanation: Paul Cézanne's geometric approach to painting — reducing forms to cylinders, spheres, and cones, and showing multiple viewpoints simultaneously — directly influenced Cubism. Picasso and Braque acknowledged Cézanne as their primary influence. Cézanne himself said: 'I want to make of Impressionism something solid and durable, like the art of the museums.'
facts.statement: Cézanne is called 'the father of modern art' because his geometric approach directly influenced Cubism. Picasso and Braque acknowledged him as their primary inspiration.
facts.wowFactor: Cézanne was dismissed as a failed painter for most of his life — rejected 20 times by the Paris Salon. At his first solo retrospective at age 56, Picasso said: 'He is the father of us all.' Cézanne died unaware of his influence.

facts.quizQuestion: Van Gogh created an enormous body of work in just over a decade — approximately how many artworks did he produce?
facts.explanation: Van Gogh created approximately 2,100 artworks in just over a decade — including about 860 oil paintings, mostly in the last two years of his life. He died at 37. His output was extraordinary given he only seriously took up painting around age 27.
facts.statement: Van Gogh created approximately 2,100 artworks in just over a decade, including about 860 oil paintings — most in his final two years.
facts.wowFactor: In his final 70 days at Auvers-sur-Oise, Van Gogh painted at least one canvas per day — sometimes two. He was racing against time he didn't know was running out.

facts.quizQuestion: Which artist was Van Gogh's guest in Arles in 1888, whose departure preceded the famous ear-cutting incident?
facts.explanation: Paul Gauguin stayed with Van Gogh at his Yellow House in Arles from October to December 1888. Their friendship deteriorated due to artistic and personal tensions. On December 23, 1888, following an argument with Gauguin, Van Gogh cut off part of his own ear. Gauguin fled the next morning and the two never met again.
facts.statement: Gauguin stayed with Van Gogh in Arles in late 1888. Their argument on December 23 preceded Van Gogh cutting off part of his own ear.
facts.wowFactor: After the ear incident, Gauguin wrote to Van Gogh's brother Theo: 'Please write to your brother carefully, and after having talked with the doctor, I dare not leave him at this time, though I am compelled to.' He still left the next morning.

facts.quizQuestion: Pablo Picasso was extraordinarily prolific — approximately how many works did he produce across painting, sculpture, ceramics, and theater design?
facts.explanation: Picasso produced approximately 20,000 works across painting, sculpture, ceramics, collage, and theater design across his 91 years. He co-founded Cubism, invented constructed sculpture, and co-invented collage. He worked across multiple styles and movements throughout his life.
facts.statement: Picasso produced approximately 20,000 works across painting, sculpture, ceramics, and theater design across his 91-year life.
facts.wowFactor: Picasso was so prolific that authenticating his work became a career in itself — the Picasso Administration reviews thousands of attribution claims each year, rejecting most as forgeries or misattributions.

facts.quizQuestion: Abstract Expressionism had two main tendencies — what are they?
facts.explanation: Abstract Expressionism had two main tendencies: 'action painting' (gestural — Pollock, de Kooning, Kline), characterized by energetic, physical paint application; and 'color field' painting (Rothko, Newman, Still), characterized by large areas of flat, unmodulated color evoking meditative mood.
facts.statement: Abstract Expressionism had two tendencies: 'action painting' (Pollock, de Kooning — gestural energy) and 'color field' (Rothko, Newman — large areas of flat color).
facts.wowFactor: Mark Rothko's Color Field paintings — simple rectangular bands of color — made gallery visitors weep. Rothko said: 'If you are only moved by color relationships, you are missing the point. I am interested in expressing basic human emotions.'

facts.quizQuestion: Surrealism aimed to express which source of images — accessing dreamlike states and irrational thoughts through art?
facts.explanation: Surrealism aimed to express the unconscious mind through art, allowing irrational, dreamlike imagery to surface without rational censorship. Founded by André Breton (Surrealist Manifesto, 1924), Surrealism was influenced by Freud's theories of the unconscious. Key techniques included automatic writing and spontaneous drawing.
facts.statement: Surrealism (1920s–50s) aimed to express the unconscious mind — using dreamlike imagery, irrationality, and automatic techniques inspired by Freud.
facts.wowFactor: Surrealists used 'automatic writing' — writing or drawing without conscious control — to access the unconscious. Dalí had his own method: he held a key over a plate, letting himself fall asleep so it would drop and wake him the moment he reached a dream state.

facts.quizQuestion: Which museum — home to the Mona Lisa and Winged Victory of Samothrace — is the world's most visited art museum?
facts.explanation: The Louvre in Paris is the world's most visited art museum, with approximately 9 million visitors per year (pre-pandemic). It holds over 550,000 works, of which about 35,000 are on display. It became a public museum in 1793. In addition to the Mona Lisa, it houses the Venus de Milo and Winged Victory of Samothrace.
facts.statement: The Louvre in Paris is the world's most visited art museum (~9 million visitors/year), holding over 550,000 works including the Mona Lisa.
facts.wowFactor: The Louvre's glass pyramid entrance — designed by I. M. Pei and opened in 1989 — was intensely controversial at first. Critics called it a 'jewel of ignorance.' Today it is considered an architectural masterpiece.

facts.quizQuestion: In which Italian city is the Uffizi Gallery — home to Botticelli's Birth of Venus and Primavera?
facts.explanation: The Uffizi Gallery (Galleria degli Uffizi) is in Florence, Italy. Built by Giorgio Vasari for Cosimo I de' Medici in 1560, it became a public museum in 1769. It holds one of the world's finest collections of Italian Renaissance art, including Botticelli's Birth of Venus and Primavera, and works by Leonardo, Michelangelo, Raphael, and Titian.
facts.statement: The Uffizi Gallery is in Florence, Italy — one of the world's greatest repositories of Italian Renaissance art, including Botticelli's Birth of Venus.
facts.wowFactor: The Uffizi was originally Cosimo de' Medici's government office building (Uffizi = offices) — the art collection was kept upstairs as a private family museum. It became public in 1769 when the last Medici heir left the collection to Florence.

facts.quizQuestion: In which Dutch city is the Rijksmuseum — home to Rembrandt's The Night Watch?
facts.explanation: The Rijksmuseum is in Amsterdam, Netherlands. It is the national museum of the Netherlands, dedicated to Dutch arts and history. Its most famous work is Rembrandt's The Night Watch (1642). The museum holds about 8,000 objects on display from a collection of one million.
