facts.statement: The Rijksmuseum is in Amsterdam, Netherlands — home to Rembrandt's The Night Watch and many other Dutch Golden Age masterpieces.
facts.wowFactor: When the Rijksmuseum planned to build a bicycle path through it, 2 million Dutch citizens signed a petition to block it. The Night Watch has its own dedicated room where it has hung since 1885.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in New York — home to Van Gogh's Starry Night and Dalí's Persistence of Memory — founded?
facts.explanation: The Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) was founded in 1929 in New York City. It opened to the public on November 7, 1929 — just 8 days after the Wall Street Crash. It holds one of the world's premier collections of modern art, including Van Gogh's Starry Night, Dalí's Persistence of Memory, Mondrian's Broadway Boogie Woogie, and Warhol's Campbell's Soup Cans.
facts.statement: MoMA (Museum of Modern Art) was founded in New York in 1929 — opening just 8 days after the Wall Street Crash.
facts.wowFactor: MoMA opened on November 7, 1929 — just 8 days after the Wall Street Crash. While the financial world collapsed, the museum opened to host the future of art.

facts.quizQuestion: Klimt's The Kiss and Alphonse Mucha's decorative posters are examples of which ornate movement (c. 1890–1910) characterized by flowing organic lines?
facts.explanation: Art Nouveau (French: 'new art') was a decorative style flourishing c. 1890–1910, characterized by flowing organic lines, natural forms, and ornate ornamentation. In German-speaking countries it was called Jugendstil. Key artists include Klimt, Alphonse Mucha, and Egon Schiele. It influenced architecture (Gaudí), jewelry, and graphic design.
facts.statement: Art Nouveau (c. 1890–1910) is characterized by flowing organic lines and ornate decoration — seen in Klimt's The Kiss and Mucha's posters.
facts.wowFactor: Art Nouveau was the first truly international art style — simultaneously flourishing in Paris, Vienna, Brussels, Barcelona, Glasgow, Prague, and New York. It was killed off almost instantly by WWI and the modernist backlash.

facts.quizQuestion: Who was Van Gogh's younger brother — an art dealer in Paris who financially supported Vincent throughout his career and preserved his letters?
facts.explanation: Theo van Gogh was Vincent's younger brother and primary financial supporter throughout his career. Theo worked as an art dealer in Paris and sent money to Vincent every month. He preserved Vincent's letters — over 800 to Theo alone — which are the primary source for understanding Van Gogh's life and art. Theo died just six months after Vincent.
facts.statement: Theo van Gogh — Vincent's younger brother and art dealer — financially supported him throughout his career and preserved over 800 letters.
facts.wowFactor: Theo van Gogh died just six months after his brother Vincent — possibly from grief and the stress of managing Vincent's affairs. He never saw his brother become famous.

facts.quizQuestion: Degas is famous for his oil paintings, but in his later career — as his eyesight failed — which drawing medium did he increasingly rely on?
facts.explanation: As Degas suffered increasing eye disease in his later career, he turned increasingly to pastel — a drawing medium he could work with more closely and precisely than oil paint. Many of his most famous dancer images are pastels. He also turned to sculpture, creating wax models (including the Little Dancer Aged Fourteen) that could be touched and felt.
facts.statement: As Degas's eyesight failed in later life, he increasingly turned to pastel drawing and sculpture rather than oil painting.
facts.wowFactor: Degas's Little Dancer Aged Fourteen sculpture — the only sculpture he exhibited in his lifetime — was originally dressed in a real tutu, hair ribbon, and ballet slippers. It shocked critics in 1881 for its uncomfortable realism.

facts.quizQuestion: Edvard Munch created how many versions of The Scream?
facts.explanation: Munch created four known versions of The Scream: two in oil (1893 and 1910), one in pastel (1895), and one in crayon (1895). The primary oil version is at the National Museum of Norway. The 1895 pastel version sold for $119.9 million at Sotheby's in 2012.
facts.statement: Munch created four versions of The Scream (two oils, one pastel, one crayon). The 1895 pastel sold for $119.9 million in 2012.
facts.wowFactor: The $119.9 million Scream sale in 2012 was conducted in English — rare for a Sotheby's New York auction of a Norwegian work. The bidding took 12 minutes; the winning bidder stayed anonymous.

facts.quizQuestion: J. M. W. Turner — often cited as a precursor to Impressionism for his atmospheric landscapes — was from which country?
facts.explanation: Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) was a British painter from London. He specialized in atmospheric landscapes and marine paintings that dissolved forms in light and mist. Monet and the French Impressionists were influenced by his technique. His paintings are mainly in the National Gallery and Tate in London.
facts.statement: J. M. W. Turner was British (from London), celebrated for atmospheric landscapes that dissolve forms in light — influencing the French Impressionists.
facts.wowFactor: Turner left nearly 300 paintings and 30,000 watercolors to the British nation in his will — on condition they be kept together and displayed for free. The Tate still holds them all.

facts.quizQuestion: Many art historians believe Vermeer used which optical device — projecting a scene onto a surface — to achieve his extraordinary precision and photo-realistic detail?
facts.explanation: Most art historians believe Vermeer used a camera obscura — an optical device that projects an image of the outside world onto an interior surface using a lens — to achieve the precision and tonal accuracy of his paintings. The theory gained support from Tim Jenison's 2013 documentary 'Tim's Vermeer.' The camera obscura was also studied by Leonardo da Vinci.
facts.statement: Many art historians believe Vermeer used a camera obscura to project scenes onto his canvas, explaining his extraordinary precision and photographic detail.
facts.wowFactor: Tim Jenison, an inventor with no painting experience, built a replica of Vermeer's studio and camera obscura setup and recreated The Music Lesson in perfect detail — proving the technique could produce Vermeer-level accuracy without artistic talent.

facts.quizQuestion: Before the rise of private collectors, which institution was the dominant patron of Western art — commissioning most major paintings, frescoes, and sculptures from the Medieval period through the Baroque?
facts.explanation: The Catholic Church was the dominant patron of Western art from the Medieval period through the Baroque. It commissioned the Sistine Chapel ceiling, Raphael's Vatican Stanze, Caravaggio's chapel paintings, and countless altarpieces and sculptures. Church patronage defined the subjects and style of Western art for over a thousand years.
facts.statement: The Catholic Church was the dominant patron of Western art from the Medieval period through the Baroque, commissioning the Sistine Chapel, Raphael's Vatican works, and Caravaggio's paintings.
facts.wowFactor: The Catholic Church rejected several of Caravaggio's commissioned paintings as too realistic — his saints had dirty feet and looked like common people. Two of his rejected works can still be seen today in Roman churches that accepted them.

facts.quizQuestion: In which Spanish city is the Prado Museum — home to Velázquez's Las Meninas and Bosch's Garden of Earthly Delights?
facts.explanation: The Museo del Prado is in Madrid, Spain. Founded in 1819, it is one of the world's finest art museums and the preeminent repository of Spanish art. Its collection includes masterpieces by Velázquez, Goya, El Greco, Rubens, Titian, and Bosch. Velázquez's Las Meninas and Goya's Third of May 1808 are its most famous works.
facts.statement: The Prado Museum is in Madrid, Spain — one of the world's great museums, with masterworks by Velázquez, Goya, Bosch, and Titian.
facts.wowFactor: The Prado holds 140 Goya paintings — more than any other museum — collected over his lifetime as Spain's court painter. The 'Black Paintings' he painted on his own house walls were transferred to canvas and now hang there.
